title: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm
authors: William H. Sauer, Paul C. Zei

items.subtopic: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA
items.title: Identifying Normal vs. Pathological Sinus Arrhythmia
items.content: Sinus arrhythmia is a common, benign condition characterized by irregular P-P intervals greater than 120 ms originating from the sinus node. It is typically asymptomatic and related to normal physiological processes such as respiration or vagal tone changes. Differentiating it from pathological causes like premature atrial contractions (PACs) involves recognizing consistent P-wave morphology.
keyPoints: Sinus arrhythmia: irregular P-P intervals >120 ms, sinus node origin
Types of sinus arrhythmia: respirophasic, ventriculophasic, non-phasic
Respirophasic: related to respiratory cycle and vagal tone changes
Ventriculophasic: associated with heart block or PVCs
Nonphasic: variations unrelated to cardiac/respiratory cycles
Asymptomatic sinus arrhythmia does not require further evaluation
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA

items.subtopic: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA
items.title: Characteristics of Normal Sinus Rhythm on ECG
items.content: Normal sinus rhythm is identified by a heart rate between 60 and 100 beats per minute, with P waves originating from the superior and lateral aspect of the right atrium. The frontal plane axis directs inferiorly and leftward, showing positive P waves in leads II, III, and aVF; negative in aVR; and biphasic morphology in lead V1.
keyPoints: Normal sinus rhythm: 60-100 beats/min
P wave origin: superior/lateral right atrium
Frontal plane axis: inferiorly/leftward
Positive P waves: leads II, III, aVF
Negative P wave: lead aVR
Biphasic P wave morphology in lead V1
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA
items.title: Distinguishing Physiologic from Nonphysiologic Sinus Tachycardia
items.content: Physiologic sinus tachycardia occurs as an appropriate response to stimuli like exercise or stress, with gradual rate changes. In contrast, nonphysiologic sinus tachycardia, such as inappropriate sinus tachycardia, involves spontaneous increases at rest or disproportionate responses to exertion.
keyPoints: Sinus tachycardia is defined as >100 beats/min.
Gradual rate increase suggests physiologic sinus tachycardia; abrupt onset and offset suggest focal atrial tachycardia.
Consider underlying conditions like thyroid abnormalities or anemia in patients with sinus tachycardia.
Inappropriate sinus tachycardia often affects women in their third or fourth decade, presenting with fatigue, dizziness, palpitations, chest pain, headaches, and gastrointestinal upset.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA
items.title: Management of Inappropriate Sinus Tachycardia
items.content: Inappropriate sinus tachycardia, characterized by autonomic dysregulation and spontaneous rate increases at rest, often requires careful management. Treatment options include beta blockers, clonidine, serotonin reuptake inhibitors, and ivabradine.
keyPoints: Ivabradine blocks the $ I_{r} $ current in sinus node cells, approved for heart failure and effective for inappropriate sinus tachycardia.
Catheter ablation of the sinus node may lead to symptomatic bradycardia or pacemaker requirement.
Symptoms include fatigue, dizziness, palpitations, chest pain, headaches, and gastrointestinal upset.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA
items.title: Characteristics of Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome (POTS)
items.content: POTS is marked by a significant increase in sinus rate upon standing, often linked to autonomic dysfunction. Management includes volume expansion, medications like fludrocortisone and midodrine, and exercise training.
keyPoints: Sinus rate increases by 30 beats/min or to >120 beats/min within 10 min of standing without hypotension in POTS.
POTS may follow viral illnesses, including COVID-19, presenting with autonomic dysfunction.
Treatment includes salt supplementation, fludrocortisone, compression stockings, midodrine, and exercise training.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA
items.question: Which of the following best describes the characteristic P wave morphology in a normal sinus rhythm on an ECG?
options: Positive deflection in leads I, II, III, and biphasic morphology in lead V1.
Negative deflection in leads I, II, III, and positive morphology in lead V1.
Biphasic deflection in leads I, II, III, and negative morphology in lead V1.
Positive deflection in leads aVR, I, II, and biphasic morphology in lead V2.
items.explanation: The characteristic P wave originates from the superior and lateral aspect of the right atrium with positive deflections in leads I, II, III, and a biphasic morphology in lead V1.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA

items.subtopic: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA
items.question: Which type of sinus arrhythmia is associated with reflexive changes during the respiratory cycle?
items.explanation: Respirophasic sinus arrhythmia occurs when vagal tone changes reflexively during inspiration and expiration.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA

items.subtopic: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA
items.question: What is the typical P-P interval irregularity that defines sinus arrhythmia?
items.explanation: Sinus arrhythmia is defined by an irregularity between P-P intervals of greater than 120 ms.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA
items.question: Which of the following is a typical feature of physiologic sinus tachycardia as compared to normal sinus rhythm?
options: Shorter P waves in inferior limb leads
Taller P waves in inferior limb leads
Decreased rate of spontaneous depolarization of the sinus node
Focus of earliest activation shifts rightward
items.explanation: In physiologic sinus tachycardia, the focus of earliest activation within the sinus node typically shifts more leftward and closer to the superior septal aspect of the crista terminalis, producing taller P waves in the inferior limb leads.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA
items.question: What is a distinguishing feature between sinus tachycardia and focal atrial tachycardia?
options: Abrupt onset and offset of tachycardia
Tall P waves in inferior leads
items.explanation: A causative factor (e.g., exertion) and a gradual rate increase favor a diagnosis of sinus tachycardia, whereas abrupt onset and offset favor atrial tachycardia.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA
items.question: In which condition is the sinus rate increase considered inappropriate?
options: At rest or out of proportion to exertion
items.explanation: Inappropriate sinus tachycardia is characterized by an increase in sinus rate at rest or out of proportion to physiologic stress or exertion.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA
items.question: Which treatment is approved in the United States for heart failure and has been effective in treating inappropriate sinus tachycardia?
items.explanation: Ivabradine, a drug that blocks the $ I_{r} $ current causing spontaneous sinus node depolarization, is approved in the United States for use in heart failure and has been effective in treating inappropriate sinus tachycardia.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA
items.statement: Sinus arrhythmia is considered pathogenic and warrants further cardiac evaluation.
items.explanation: Asymptomatic sinus arrhythmia does not warrant further cardiac evaluation and is not considered pathogenic.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA

items.subtopic: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA
items.statement: Ventriculophasic sinus arrhythmia can be observed after a premature ventricular contraction.
items.explanation: Ventriculophasic sinus arrhythmia is often seen with heart block or after a premature ventricular contraction.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA
items.statement: Sinus bradycardia is defined as a heart rate greater than 100 beats/min.
items.explanation: Sinus bradycardia is defined as rates <60 beats/min.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA
items.statement: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) is characterized by a sinus rate increase of 30 beats/min or to >120 beats/min within 10 minutes of standing.
items.explanation: POTS involves symptomatic sinus tachycardia with an increase in heart rate by 30 beats/min or to >120 beats/min upon standing.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA
items.assertion: Sinus arrhythmia is often eliminated with exertion.
items.reason: Exertion removes vagal tone, which contributes to the irregularity seen at rest.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why sinus arrhythmia is often eliminated with exertion.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm | SINUS ARRHYTHMIA

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA
items.assertion: Inappropriate sinus tachycardia is often associated with autonomic dysregulation.
items.reason: The underlying mechanism may be related to an imbalance between sympathetic and parasympathetic inputs to the sinus node.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA
items.assertion: Catheter ablation of the sinus node is a highly effective long-term treatment for inappropriate sinus tachycardia.
items.reason: It can result in symptomatic sinus bradycardia or arrest, leading to a permanent pacemaker requirement.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because catheter ablation often results in poor long-term control of symptoms and may require a permanent pacemaker.
items.reference: Physiologic and Nonphysiologic Sinus Rhythm · PHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA | NONPHYSIOLOGIC SINUS TACHYCARDIA
